Reserve this for abilities that represent a full commitment — the character is doing this and nothing else this turn. Should have significant impact to justify the cost. Example: Arise (animate corpses), True Possession (spirit projection).
abilityTypes.gambit: An optional technique layered onto an existing action (Attack, Dodge, Endure, or a specific maneuver). Costs -1 Tier from the primary result. Declared before rolling. Must reach Masterful to activate (no gambiting from Fleeting). Vocation gambits follow the same rules as discipline gambits — they are not special. A vocation gambit that modifies Attack should specify the weapon type or context it applies to. A vocation gambit that modifies a vocation maneuver should reference that maneuver by name.
abilityTypes.exploit: A new way to spend the Exploit pip. When an enemy presents an Opening, the character may spend their Exploit pip to take an offensive reaction. Vocation exploit abilities modify or replace this default Exploit reaction — changing what happens when the character seizes an Opening. The Exploit pip is a per-round resource (1 per round by default), so exploit abilities are inherently self-limiting. Defenses are free and never cost the Exploit pip.
abilityTypes.permission: Grants the character the ability to do something the rules normally prohibit. No roll, no cost — the character simply can. Permissions are the rarest ability type and should be reserved for capstone-level effects that represent absolute mastery. The permission should be narrow enough that it does not trivialize entire encounter types. Example: Ghost's Exit Strategy (permission to escape a scene under specific conditions).

interactionWithDisciplines.overview: Vocations and disciplines are parallel systems. Disciplines provide gambits — optional techniques that fire on action rolls. Vocations provide identity-level abilities that change how the character approaches situations. The two systems should complement, not compete.
interactionWithDisciplines.substitutionRule: When a vocation grants a discipline substitution (use Discipline A in place of Discipline B), the character uses Discipline A's die and Discipline A's gambits in that context. They lose access to Discipline B's gambits for that roll. This is a trade, not a free upgrade. A Noble who uses Charm to intimidate has access to Disarming Smile, Silver Tongue, and Sway — not Menace, Crack, and Break. The player must evaluate whether their Charm gambits serve the situation better than the Intimidate gambits would have. If the substitution always produces better results, the design has failed.
interactionWithDisciplines.redundancyCheck: Before finalizing any vocation ability, compare it against the discipline gambits available in the vocation's favored discipline and the discipline being substituted (if any). If a vocation ability does something a discipline gambit already does at the same cost, one of them is redundant. The vocation ability should do something the gambit system cannot — change scope (individual to group), change timing (reactive to proactive), change information flow (mechanical to narrative), or change the fiction (what the action looks like in the world).
interactionWithDisciplines.gambitsFromVocations: Vocation gambits follow all standard gambit rules: -1 Tier cost, declared before rolling, minimum Masterful result to activate. They are not 'better gambits' — they are gambits with a different thematic source. A vocation gambit should feel like it comes from the character's lived experience, not from their training manual. A Gunslinger's Fan the Hammer is a reflex pattern burned into muscle memory. A Shockboxer's Body Work is ring experience — knowing exactly where to place a shot to accelerate the punishment.

conditionInteraction.overview: Vocations frequently grant, require, or interact with the condition system. Understanding how conditions stack (they do not) is critical to balancing vocation abilities.
conditionInteraction.noConditionStacking: Named conditions do not stack. A character who is [Optimized] from three different sources is still just [Optimized]. The Control die steps up once, not three times. Multiple sources of the same condition provide redundancy (overlapping durations, different triggers) but never amplification. This is the single most important balance rule for vocations. Design accordingly — granting [Optimized] from multiple vocation abilities is acceptable because it provides coverage, not power.
conditionInteraction.unnamedBonusesStack: Unnamed mechanical bonuses (+1 Tier, step up Effect, etc.) do stack with each other and with conditions. A +1 Tier from Read the Room and a +1 Tier from Follow the Thread are separate unnamed bonuses and both apply. This is intentional — these bonuses are earned through specific actions and tier investments, not passively accumulated.
conditionInteraction.durationOverlap: When a character has the same condition from multiple sources with different durations, all durations track independently. The condition is present as long as at least one duration remains active. When the last duration expires, the condition clears. This means multiple sources extend coverage but never double the effect.

balanceFramework.overview: Vocation balance is not about mathematical equivalence between vocations. It is about ensuring each vocation delivers on its identity without invalidating another vocation's identity or making the base discipline system irrelevant.
balanceFramework.domainRule: Each vocation should dominate one domain — Combat, Social, Narrative, or Force. Dominance means the vocation is clearly the best choice for that activity within its specific approach. A Noble should be the best at social pressure through charm. A Gunslinger should be the best at pistol combat. Neither should be the best at the other's domain. When a vocation grants minor benefits outside its domain (Investigator's Confrontation granting combat bonuses on Join Battle), those benefits should be reactive, costly, or conditional — never free.
balanceFramework.actionEconomyBudget: A character gets 1 Action, 1 Maneuver, and 1 Exploit per round. A vocation's abilities must fit within this budget. If a vocation grants a maneuver at Tier 2, a different maneuver at Tier 3, and a gambit that modifies an action at Tier 4, the character still only gets one of those per turn. Action economy is the natural limiter — respect it. If a vocation ability grants a free action (Follow the Thread's free Assess), it should be conditional (requires Masterful 8+ on the triggering roll) and scoped (questions must relate to the triggering context).
balanceFramework.investmentCurve: Tier 1-2 should be useful immediately but not dominant. A character with two tiers in a vocation should feel like they are learning a trade. Tier 3 is the breakpoint — the character is now defined by the vocation. Tier 4-5 are mastery rewards. The power curve should be concave — each tier adds less raw power and more refined capability. Tier 5 should not be strictly 'stronger' than Tier 3; it should be 'more complete.'
balanceFramework.multiVocationBalance: Characters may have multiple vocations. The system balances this through two mechanisms: (1) Conditions do not stack, so overlapping [Optimized] sources provide coverage, not amplification. (2) Action economy forces choices — a character with Gunslinger and Shockboxer must decide each round whether to shoot or punch, not both. When evaluating a new vocation, check its two-vocation synergies with every existing vocation. The question is not 'can a character with both be strong?' — the answer is always yes. The question is 'does a character with both bypass a cost or constraint that either vocation alone imposes?' If yes, one of the abilities needs a tighter scope.
balanceFramework.failureStates: Every ability that produces a positive outcome must have a meaningful failure state or limitation. Rolled abilities have risk text defining what happens on failure. Passives have activation conditions that are not always met. Capstones have per-scene or per-act limits. Permissions have preconditions. If an ability has no failure state, it is undercosted. The failure state should be thematic — a Noble who fails Compel faces social backlash (immune for the rest of the scene), not mechanical damage. A Ghost who fails Slip presents an Opening — they tried to be clever and got caught.
balanceFramework.gmLoadTest: Run every ability through the GM load test: can the GM adjudicate this ability quickly at the table without consulting the rulebook? If the ability requires tracking three different states, remembering a trigger from two rounds ago, and cross-referencing another player's condition, it is too complex. Simplify the trigger, reduce the tracking, or make the duration shorter. The best vocation abilities are ones where the player declares, the GM nods, and the game continues.

investigationTargetRule.overview: Some vocations reference a 'target of investigation' or similar ongoing focus. This is a player-declared narrative commitment, not a GM designation.
investigationTargetRule.declaration: The player declares their investigation — a specific crime, conspiracy, mystery, or event they are pursuing. 'The murder of Horatio.' 'The darkside user who crushed Phil.' 'The missing shipment from Kessel.' The target is the case, not the suspect. The player may not know who is responsible — that is the point.
investigationTargetRule.scope: Abilities that reference the investigation target apply to anyone the character encounters in connection with that investigation — witnesses, suspects, accomplices, victims, locations. The GM adjudicates whether a given interaction is connected. In practice, this is almost always obvious in context.
investigationTargetRule.limits: One active investigation at a time. The player may close an investigation and open a new one between scenes. Closing an investigation does not erase learned information — it simply redirects the mechanical benefits.

socialVocationGuidelines.overview: Social vocations (Noble, and future social archetypes) operate in narrative space where GM adjudication is the primary resolution mechanism. Social abilities must be designed with clear boundaries so the GM knows what they are being asked to permit.
socialVocationGuidelines.autoWinAbilities: Some abilities are designed to auto-resolve minor social needs without a roll. This is intentional — a Noble should not have to roll dice to get a dockworker to carry their bags. The auto-win boundary is plausibility within the fiction. If the NPC has no reason to refuse and the request is minor, it succeeds. The GM's only check is: is there an active reason this person would refuse (hostility, fear, conflicting loyalty)? If not, it works. This avoids creating new NPC stat blocks for trivial interactions.
socialVocationGuidelines.compelAndResistance: Social pressure abilities (Compel, Intimidate replacements) must always be rolled against the target's Resolve. No social ability bypasses Resolve entirely — that is what Resolve exists for. The escalation path is: Tier 1 effects make the target uncomfortable, Tier 2 effects force a choice (comply or justify), Tier 3 effects force compliance with attitude shift. Even at Tier 3, the target complies with the specific request — they are not mind-controlled.
socialVocationGuidelines.failureImmunity: When a social ability fails against a target, that target should become immune to that specific ability from that character for the rest of the scene. This prevents players from spamming social rolls until one succeeds. The immunity is specific — failing Compel against someone does not make them immune to Read the Room. The character can still gather information, they just cannot pressure the target again with the same tool.

narrativeVocationGuidelines.overview: Narrative vocations (Investigator, and future knowledge/exploration archetypes) generate information rather than mechanical effects. Information is the hardest resource to balance because its value depends entirely on context.
narrativeVocationGuidelines.gmConfirmationRule: Narrative abilities that reconstruct events, propose theories, or reveal hidden information must include a GM confirmation step. The GM confirms that the target is narratively significant before the roll occurs. If there is nothing to find, the ability does not activate and the action cost is not spent. This prevents players from sweep-searching every room and it gives the GM control over pacing.
narrativeVocationGuidelines.theoryProposal: When an ability allows a player to propose a theory (Connect the Dots pattern), the GM has three responses: confirm (correct), partially confirm (adjust to truth), or deny (wrong). The GM must always respond — silence is not an option. A partial confirmation that adjusts the theory to the truth is the most powerful response because it gives the player correct information while preserving GM narrative control. The player is never punished for proposing a wrong theory — the ability's value is in forcing a response.
narrativeVocationGuidelines.informationVsPower: Narrative abilities should never directly grant combat bonuses as their primary effect. When combat bonuses appear (The Confrontation), they should be conditional on the investigation reaching a specific narrative inflection point and should cost a resource (Edge). The vocation's power is in knowing — not in fighting. Combat benefits are a side effect of preparation, not the purpose of the vocation.

forceVocationGuidelines.overview: Force vocations (Ichor Witch, and future Force archetypes) channel the Spark disciplines through a specific cultural or philosophical lens. They are the most narratively dramatic vocations and the most mechanically dangerous to balance.
forceVocationGuidelines.darkSideCost: Force abilities that produce dramatic, reality-bending effects must have a dark side cost. This is not a mechanical tax — it is a narrative consequence expressed through the Hope/Toll tracker. Flipping Hope to Toll changes the Destiny Pool's passive bonuses for the entire crew. The player is not just spending their own resources — they are shifting the table's moral ledger. This cost should only appear on abilities that are genuinely transgressive within the fiction (possession, necromancy, mind control).
forceVocationGuidelines.sustainCosts: Powerful Force abilities that persist across rounds should require Edge expenditure to sustain. This creates a natural timer — the character runs out of Edge and the effect ends. The sustain cost should be listed clearly in the ability text so the player can budget. Sustain costs also create tactical decisions: do I spend Edge to maintain this ability, or do I save it for a reroll?
forceVocationGuidelines.bodyVulnerability: Force abilities that project the character's consciousness or attention elsewhere should leave the body in a vulnerable state ([Helpless], [Disoriented]). This creates a meaningful risk and encourages the party to protect the Force user — it is a team game, not a solo power fantasy.

commonPitfalls: Stacking Optimized — If multiple abilities in the same vocation grant [Optimized], the vocation is wasting design space. Multiple [Optimized] sources provide redundancy but never power. Spread your condition grants across different conditions ([Rattled], [Exposed], [Optimized], [Empowered]) for maximum tactical variety.

Capstones That Never Fire — A capstone with preconditions outside the player's control (all allies must have concealment, the target must be at less than half Vitality, the moon must be in the right phase) will never produce a memorable moment. The player should be able to engineer the preconditions through deliberate play, ideally using the vocation's lower-tier abilities.

Maneuver Flooding — A vocation with three maneuvers gives the character three things they can do with their Maneuver pip. This sounds like options, but in practice it creates decision paralysis and means two of those maneuvers rarely see use. One maneuver (Tier 2 or 3) that grows through upgrades (Tier 4-5) is almost always better design than three separate maneuvers.

Passive Creep — Passives are convenient because they do not require action economy. But a vocation with four passives and one maneuver feels like a stat block, not an identity. Players engage with abilities they actively use. Balance passive and active abilities — at minimum, a vocation should have at least two tiers that require a decision from the player each round.

Discipline Erasure — A vocation that replaces so many disciplines that the original discipline system becomes irrelevant for that character has gone too far. Discipline substitutions should be narrow: one discipline replaced in one specific context. If a vocation's Tier 1 lets the character use their favored discipline for everything, the five-arena system has been undermined.

Free Action Cascades — Abilities that grant free actions (free Assess, free attack, free maneuver) must be conditional and scoped. An unconditional free action every round is equivalent to doubling the character's action economy, which breaks encounter balance. Free actions should require a high roll threshold (Masterful 8+ on Control), cost a resource, or impose a scope restriction on what the free action can target.

Narrative Abilities Without GM Hooks — Any ability that generates narrative information must give the GM a clear hook for what to reveal and how much. 'Learn something useful' is not a design. 'Learn the most recent significant event involving the subject — who was here, what they did, approximately when' is a design. The GM should be able to read the ability text and immediately know what to narrate.

name: Scale (Starship)
rule: When ships of different sizes engage, Scale modifies the dice. Larger target = step up attacker's Control die, step down attacker's Power die (easier to hit, harder to damage meaningfully). Smaller target = step down attacker's Control die, step up attacker's Power die (harder to hit, devastating if it connects). Per step of Scale difference: +1 step to Control die toward the larger target, -1 step to Power die. Reverse when targeting smaller.
guide: A starfighter can't casually scratch a Star Destroyer. But if a Star Destroyer manages to track a starfighter — one shot. Scale makes size matter the way it should.

name: Hardware Die
rule: In starship combat, the Power die for each roll is determined by the ship's hardware systems (Handling, Engines, Shields, Sensors, Fire Control), not the character's personal arena die. A character's discipline die still serves as the Control die. The hardware die replaces the arena die as the Power source.
guide: Your personal strength doesn't matter when you're pulling the trigger on a quad laser. What matters is how powerful the quad laser is. That's the Hardware Die — the ship's stats, not yours.

name: Station (Starship)
rule: Each crew member occupies one station during starship combat: Pilot, Gunner, Operator, Engineer, or Co-Pilot. A station defines which discipline provides the Control die and which hardware system provides the Power die. Each station has its own set of available actions and reactions.
guide: Everyone has a job on the ship. The Pilot flies. The Gunner shoots. The Operator sees. The Engineer fixes. The Co-Pilot makes everyone better. Pick your seat and do your job.

name: Integrity
rule: Integrity is the ship's equivalent of Vitality. When a ship takes hull damage, Integrity decreases. At 0 Integrity, the ship is destroyed or disabled (GM determines based on narrative context).
guide: Integrity is how much punishment the hull can take before the ship stops being a ship and starts being debris. Watch the number. When it hits zero, you'd better be near an escape pod.

name: Hull Rating
rule: Hull Rating is the soak value per impairment. Each system impairment absorbs UP TO Hull Rating points of damage — excess is wasted. On a Hull 2 ship, each impairment absorbs exactly 2 damage. Capital ships with high Hull Ratings soak fully per hit, but must spend entire impairments even on small hits — swarm tactics exploit this by forcing multiple small impairments that each waste soak capacity.
guide: The hull soaks damage in chunks. Hull Rating 2 means every impairment absorbs 2 points — no more, no less. A 1-damage hit still costs the crew a full impairment. That's why swarms of fighters can bleed a Star Destroyer dry — each potshot costs a full system tick.

name: System Impairment
rule: System impairment is the starship mirror of character trauma. Three levels: Impaired (Power die steps down 1), Debilitated (Power die steps down 2), Disabled (system unavailable, actions requiring it auto-fail). When damage exceeds Hull Rating, crew may choose to impair a system to soak excess. Without a Called Shot, the crew chooses which system.
guide: Something breaks to save the ship. That's the deal. You pick which system takes the hit — unless the enemy called their shot, in which case they already decided for you.

name: Called Shot (Starship)
rule: When an attacker uses the Called Shot action, they designate which system must take the impairment if the defending crew chooses to impair to soak damage. Without a Called Shot, the defending crew chooses which system to impair. At Legendary, the designated system takes one extra step of impairment.
guide: You point at their engines and say 'that one.' If they take the hit and need to soak, it's the engines that go. Precision violence.

name: Evade (Starship)
rule: The ship's Dodge equivalent. The Pilot rolls Piloting (Reflex) + Handling to evade incoming fire. On success, the Power die result reduces the attacker's Effect Tier. On failure, no mitigation — full effect lands. On Control 8+, the evasion generates an Opening for Gunners.
guide: Jink, roll, change vector. If you're good, the shot misses entirely. If you're great, the evasion opens up a shot for your Gunners. If you fail — well, the shields better hold.

name: Angle Deflector Shields
rule: The ship's Endure equivalent. The Operator angles the shield envelope to absorb incoming fire — Tech (Wits) Control, Shields Power die. On success, the Shields die result reduces the attacker's Effect Tier. On failure, reduces the attacker's Effect Tier by 1 — the shields always provide a minimum floor of protection, identical to character Endure. This is the baseline ship defense, available against any attack type. The Operator declares 'Angle deflector shields!' and the shields rotate to catch the incoming fire.
guide: The Operator's shield defense. Angle the shields, absorb the hit. Even when the angle is wrong, the shields bleed off some energy. It's never nothing.

name: Resist (Starship)
rule: Defense against electronic and ion attacks, Called Shots targeting systems, and jamming attempts. The Sensors die defends against non-physical threats. On failure, reduces the attacker's Effect Tier by 1 — same floor as Angle Deflector Shields.
guide: Electronic countermeasures. Your ship's immune system against ion bolts, jamming, and system intrusion. Even when it fails, it slows the attack down.

name: Engaged (Starship Range)
rule: Ships are in extremely close proximity — docking range, collision risk, boarding possible. Rare in open space combat. Equivalent to personal Engaged zone at starship scale.
guide: Hull-to-hull. You can see the rivets on their plating. This is either a boarding action or a very bad day.

name: Close (Starship Range)
rule: Standard combat range. Most weapons are effective. Maneuvering matters — Attack Runs and Evasive Maneuvers operate primarily at this range band.
guide: Normal fighting distance. Close enough to shoot, far enough to dodge. This is where most dogfights happen.

name: Far (Starship Range)
rule: Long range engagement. Only long-range weapons (turbolasers, torpedoes) are effective at this range. Ships must Fly to close the gap before entering a Dogfight.
guide: Barely visible on the scope. You need big guns or guided ordnance to touch anything out here. This is where chases happen.

name: Fire Control
rule: The ship's unified weapons management system. Fire Control is a single hardware Power die (D4–D12) that governs ALL weapon output. Individual weapons define chassis (damage table), arc, range, and traits — but the Power die comes from Fire Control, not the weapon itself. Impairing Fire Control degrades ALL weapon output simultaneously. A Called Shot to Fire Control is the most efficient way to cripple a ship's offensive capability.
guide: One system runs all the guns. When Fire Control is hot, every weapon on the ship hits harder. When it's damaged, every turret and torpedo tube suffers. One system, one throat to choke.

name: Firing Arc
rule: The directional coverage of a weapon mount. Forward: only targets ahead of the ship. Dorsal: covers the top hemisphere. Ventral: covers the bottom hemisphere. Turret: full 360-degree coverage. A mount can only fire at targets within its arc. The Pilot's positioning determines which arcs bear on the enemy.
guide: Guns don't swivel in every direction. Forward guns shoot forward. Turrets shoot everywhere. Where the Pilot points the ship determines which guns can fire.

name: [Elusive]
fiction: The ship is actively evading — jinking, rolling, changing vector unpredictably. Targeting solutions decay before they can be used.
rule: Applied by the Pilot's Evasive Maneuvers action. All incoming attacks against the ship have their Control die stepped down. Duration: until start of the Pilot's next turn. Does not stack with itself.
guide: The ship is dancing. Every targeting solution the enemy computes is obsolete before they can pull the trigger.

name: [Jammed]
fiction: The ship's sensor suite is flooded with garbage data. Every screen is showing false returns, every instrument is unreliable. The crew cannot trust what they are seeing — their judgment is compromised because the information they are working from is corrupted.
rule: Applied by the Operator's Jam action. On PC ship: crew gains [Rattled] — steps down the Wits-arena Control die for all Sensor-associated actions (Operator, Co-Pilot, Engineer actions that rely on sensor data). On NPC ship: reduce Tier by 1 when taking any actions associated with the ship's Sensors. Duration: Tactical.
pcEffect: Crew gains [Rattled] for Sensor-associated actions (Wits-arena Control die stepped down)
guide: Your screens are garbage. Your comms are static. Your targeting computer is lying to you. Someone out there is very good at electronic warfare — and your crew's judgment is compromised because they cannot trust what any instrument is telling them.

name: [Disabled]
fiction: A specific system or weapon mount has been temporarily shut down — overheated, overloaded, or mechanically locked. It is not destroyed, but it is not available until reset.
rule: Applied to a specific weapon mount or system. The affected mount/system cannot be used until the Engineer spends an action to reset it. Unlike Impairment (which degrades output), [Disabled] is binary — the system is either available or it is not.
guide: The gun is locked. The system is down. Not broken — just disabled. Someone needs to get down there and flip the right switches before it comes back.
